title: Classical shadows over symmetric spaces
authors: Rebecca Chang, Maureen Krumtünger, Martin Larocca, Maxwell West
abstract: Efficiently learning expectation values of unknown quantum states via classical shadows has become an important primitive in both theoretical and experimental aspects of quantum computation. Typically, classical shadow protocols involve randomised measurements induced by sampling uniformly randomly from a compact group, a situation which is now quite well understood. In this work we go beyond this standard assumption, studying the classical shadow protocols occasioned by sampling uniformly randomly from the so-called compact symmetric spaces. We uncover a unifying theory of such protocols, extending the extent to which the general theory of classical shadows is understood at a mathematical level. Interestingly, for the estimation of observables sampled from certain distributions we further find that some of these protocols allow for slight improvements in sample-complexity over existing shadow schemes.
summary: This paper generalizes the classical shadows technique by investigating measurement protocols where unitaries are sampled from compact symmetric spaces rather than just groups. It reveals that certain symmetric space protocols can outperform standard methods when estimating observables that are concentrated on the diagonal of the measurement basis.
main_problem: Extending the classical shadows framework beyond standard group-based sampling to include protocols based on compact symmetric spaces (G/K).
main_result: The authors establish a unifying theory for symmetric space shadows, showing they are invertible and can provide improved sample complexity for observables concentrated on the diagonal in certain regimes.
method: The study utilizes representation theory, Weingarten calculus, and the study of measurement channels to evaluate integrals over symmetric spaces and analyze the variance of estimators.
model_or_system: The model involves an unknown quantum state in a d-dimensional Hilbert space being reconstructed via random measurements sampled from the seven infinite families of type I compact symmetric spaces.
key_observables: Expectation values of quantum observables, specifically focusing on the variance of estimators for diagonal and off-diagonal components.
important_parameters: The dimension of the Hilbert space (d), the signature of the involution (s), and the scaling regime of the signature (s in O(1) vs s = cd).
main_assumptions: The study assumes sampling is performed uniformly from the compact symmetric spaces and that the measurement basis is chosen such that involutions take standard forms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares probability distributions on the Bloch sphere for different ensembles; Figure 2 compares the variance of random symmetric observables across different shadow protocols.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of symmetric space shadows, provides a mathematical framework using group theory and Weingarten calculus, classifies the resulting measurement channels, analyzes the variance in different scaling regimes, and concludes with performance comparisons.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The probability distributions on the Bloch sphere re- sulting from acting on the state |0⟩with unitaries drawn from the uniform ensembles on SU(2) and the AIII symmetric space SU(2)/S(U(1) × U(1)). Here and more generally, the invariant measure on the symmetric space G/K lifts to a non-invariant measure on the ambient group G.

FIG. 2. Variances for random (symmetric) observables of unit 2-norm on a d = 128-dimensional system, averaged over 100 instances. Here c = (p −q)/d is the (normalized) signature of Ip,q; each choice of c identifies distinct AIII and BDI protocols. When the random observables are strongly concentrated on the diagonal (with respect to the measurement basis W), AIII and BDI shadows narrowly outperform the standard unitary and orthogonal protocols, respectively. The orthogonal protocols having a variance lower than their unitary counterparts by a constant factor (of order 2) is expected for symmetric observables [6].
